name: Mathematics Simulated Learner Authenticity Workflow Artifact
population_or_context: Fifth-grade fraction reasoning simulation for preservice teacher noticing practice.
sample_size: 15 preservice teachers in an initial implementation; 1,438 talk turns; 40 inauthentic interactions used for targeted regeneration; 240 generated responses in the full comparison; eight interview participants.
methods: The study compares few-shot prompting with multi-agent, fine-tuning, and DPO approaches, then codes simulated responses for cognition and language authenticity and gathers interview-based teacher/researcher perceptions.
education_context: Elementary mathematics teacher learning and preservice-teacher practice with a simulated fifth-grade student.
response_origin: LLM-generated student-agent utterances produced by few-shot prompting, a multi-agent responder/evaluator/refiner workflow, fine-tuned GPT-4.1, and DPO-tuned GPT-4.1.
rubric_label_space: Binary or categorical authenticity judgments for cognition and language, not a learner score rubric.
label_source: Researchers with mathematics teaching experience coded authenticity; training data included prior task utterances, Khan Academy student-AI tutor chats, and elementary fraction lesson transcripts.
generation_model: gpt-4o for the multi-agent workflow; gpt-4.1-2025-04-14 for fine-tuning and DPO; GPT-4o-mini and GPT-5.2 used in automated preference-data construction.
generation_method: Comparison of few-shot prompting, a multi-agent responder/evaluator/refiner workflow, supervised fine-tuning on student utterances, and DPO using Reflexion-generated preference pairs.
prompt_strategy: Baseline few-shot role-play prompt plus workflow-specific prompts for multi-agent critique/refinement and preference-data generation.
evaluation_method: Researcher coding of cognition and language authenticity, McNemar tests, GLMM comparisons, response-time reporting, usability/effectiveness survey, and qualitative interviews.
scorer_use: No automated scorer validation; the simulation supports teacher learning and noticing practice.
real_response_comparator: Prior task interactions, student-AI tutoring chats, and elementary classroom fraction transcripts were used to identify authenticity issues and support fine-tuning; coded authenticity compared generated responses with the study's student profile and discourse expectations.
reported_metrics: All three post-prompt approaches significantly improved cognition and language authenticity over the few-shot baseline on targeted inauthentic responses; DPO had the highest descriptive full-dataset performance at 100% language authenticity and 88.7% cognition authenticity, while GLMM differences among approaches were not significant.
limitations: Teacher-learning context rather than automated scoring or operational assessment.
The study focuses on one fraction task and one simulated learner profile.
Some workflow outputs introduced tradeoffs such as verbosity, inconsistent uncertainty, latency, or limited opportunities to extend student thinking.
